title: Stereolithography additive manufacturing of photoaligned liquid crystal elastomers
abstract: A method of forming a three-dimensional structure of liquid crystal (LC) elastomers includes contacting a resin layer of LC oligomers with a layer of photoalignment material, and exposing a portion of photoalignment material to light for aligning the portion of photoalignment material in a first orientation. The LC oligomers adjacent the illuminated portion of photoalignment material align to the first orientation of the illuminated portion. The portion of aligned LC oligomers of the resin layer are cured. Operations for forming additional layers include creating a relative movement of the resin layer away from the photoalignment material, contacting another resin layer with the photoalignment material, and exposing another portion of photoalignment material to light for aligning the photoalignment material in a different orientation. The LC oligomers adjacent the illuminated portion of photoalignment material align to the different orientation, and the portion of aligned LC oligomers of the resin layer are cured.
claims:
Claims CL IMS What is claimed: 1. A method of forming a three-dimensional structure comprising liquid crystal elastomers, the method comprising: contacting a first layer of resin with a layer of photoalignment material, the resin comprising liquid crystal oligomers and a photoinitiator; exposing a first portion of the layer of photoalignment material to a first light for aligning the first portion of the layer of photoalignment material in a first orientation corresponding to a polarity of the first light, wherein the liquid crystal oligomers adjacent the illuminated first portion of the layer of photoalignment material align to the first orientation of the first portion of the layer of photoalignment material; exposing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers of the first layer of resin for curing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers thereby forming a cured portion of the first layer of resin; and forming at least one additional layer by repeating the following operations for each additional layer: creating a relative movement of the previously formed layer away from the photoalignment material, wherein the relative movement includes adding another layer of resin onto the previously formed layer; contacting the another layer of the resin with the layer of photo alignment material; exposing another portion of the layer of photoalignment material to the first light for aligning the another portion of the layer of photoalignment material in an orientation that is different than the first orientation, 

 wherein the liquid crystal oligomers adjacent the illuminated another portion of the layer of photoalignment material align to the orientation of the another portion of the layer of photoalignment material; and exposing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers of the another layer of resin for curing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers thereby forming a cured portion of the another layer of resin. The method as recited in claim 1, wherein the orientation(s) of alignment in each layer of resin correspond to a predefined pattern of an associated layer of a three- dimensional (3D) model, wherein molecules of the photoalignment material are aligned in the respective orientation corresponding to a polarity of the first light applied thereto as defined, at least in part, by a rotational orientation of the polarizer in the path of the first light. The method as recited in claim 2, wherein a polarization angle of the first light is selected to specify each orientation of alignment corresponding to the predefined pattern of an associated layer of the three-dimensional (3D) model. The method as recited in claim 3, wherein a minimum difference between the polarization angles of two respective orientations is at least one degree. The method as recited in claim 1, comprising, after exposing the first portion of the layer of photo alignment material to the first light and before curing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers of the first layer: changing a polarity of the first light; and exposing a next portion of the layer of the photoalignmcnt material to the first light for aligning the next portion of the layer of photoalignment material in a different orientation than the first orientation, 

 wherein the liquid crystal oligomers adjacent the illuminated next portion of the layer of the photoalignment material align to the different orientation of the next portion of the layer of photoalignment material. The method as recited in claim 5, wherein the next portion of the layer of photoalignment material is located by moving the layer of photoalignment material in an x-dircction and/or a y-dircction, and the x-y directions arc in a plane that is perpendicular to a z-direction, wherein the next portion of the layer of photoalignment material comprises unaligned molecules. The method as recited in claim 1, wherein at least three layers are formed, each layer having a cured portion of cured liquid crystal elastomers, the cured portions being stacked upon one another in a direction perpendicular to a plane of the layers. The method as recited in claim 7, wherein the orientation of the aligned liquid crystal elastomers in each of the cured portions is different from the respective orientations of the aligned liquid crystal elastomers in the other cured portions. The method as recited in claim 1, wherein a wavelength of the first light for aligning molecules of the photoalignment material is in a range of greater than about 390 nanometers to about 500 nanometers. The method as recited in claim 1, wherein the curing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers comprises exposing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers to a second light at a wavelength in a range of greater than about 350 nanometers to about 390 nanometers. 

The method as recited in claim 1, wherein the photoinitiator does not initiate polymerization of the resin at a wavelength of the first light. The method as recited in claim 1, wherein a composition of the photoalignment material includes at least one component selected from the group consisting of: azobenzene and an organogel. The method as recited in claim 1, comprising heating the resin to a temperature below the nematic-to-isotropic transition temperature of the liquid crystal oligomers for lowering a viscosity of the resin. The method as recited in claim 1, wherein the three-dimensional structure is formed using a lithography-based additive manufacturing system. The method as recited in claim 1, comprising using a digital micromirror device to define the portions of the layer of photoalignment material for exposure to the first light. The method as recited in claim 1, wherein each cured portion comprises at least one voxel. The method as recited in claim 1, wherein the resin includes a photoinhibitor. A product, comprising: a three-dimensional structure having a plurality of sequentially-formed layers comprised of liquid crystal elastomers, wherein the liquid crystal elastomers in a portion of a first of the layers arc substantially aligned in a predefined first orientation, 

wherein the liquid crystal elastomers in a portion of a second of the layers are substantially aligned in a predefined second orientation that is different than the first orientation, wherein each of the portions of the three-dimensional structure is characterized as exhibiting a shape change in response to a stimulus, wherein the shape change is reversible, wherein a contiguous region of aligned liquid crystal elastomers in one of the portions has a maximum dimension of less than 60 microns. The product as recited in claim 18, wherein the predefined first orientation of alignment is different than the orientation of the as-deposited alignment of the liquid crystal elastomers. The product as recited in claim 18, wherein a second portion of the first of the layers has liquid crystal elastomers aligned in a predefined third orientation that is different than the first orientation. A method of forming a three-dimensional structure comprising liquid crystal elastomers, the method comprising: contacting a first layer of resin with a layer of photoalignment material, the resin comprising liquid crystal oligomers and a photoinitiator, wherein the photoalignment material has a plurality of portions having molecules aligned according to predefined orientations, wherein the liquid crystal oligomers adjacent a first of the portions of the layer of photoalignment material align to a predefined orientation of the first portion of the layer of photoalignment material; exposing the first layer of resin to a curing light for curing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers of the first layer of resin thereby forming cured liquid crystal elastomers aligned according to the predefined orientation; and 

forming at least one additional layer of resin in contact with the layer of photoalignment material by repeating the following operations for each additional layer: creating a relative movement of the previously formed layer away from the photoalignment material; adding another layer of resin onto the previously formed layer, wherein the liquid crystal oligomers adjacent a next of the portions of the layer of photoalignmcnt material align to a predefined next orientation of the next portion of the layer of photoalignment material, wherein the next orientation is different than the first orientation; and exposing the next layer of resin to the curing light for curing the aligned liquid crystal oligomers of the next layer of resin thereby forming cured liquid crystal elastomers aligned according to the predefined next orientation. The method as recited in claim 21, comprising, before contacting the first layer of resin with the layer of photoalignment material, forming a predefined pattern of orientations of the photoalignment material, wherein forming the predefined pattern of orientations of the photoalignment material comprises: exposing the first portion of the layer of photoalignment material to an aligning light having a first polarity to align molecules of the first portion of the layer of photoalignment material in the first orientation corresponding to the first polarity of the aligning light, forming at least one additional aligned portion of the photoalignment material by repeating the following operations for each additional portion of the predefined pattern: exposing a next portion of the layer of photoalignment material to an aligning light having a second polarity to align molecules of the 

 next portion of the layer of photoalignment material in an orientation that is different than the first orientation. The method as recited in claim 22, wherein the orientation of alignment in each portion of the layer of photoalignment material corresponds to a predefined pattern of an associated layer of a three-dimensional (3D) model, wherein molecules of the photoalignment material are aligned in the respective orientation corresponding to a polarity of the aligning light applied thereto as defined, at least in part, by a rotational orientation of the polarizer in the path of the aligning light. The method as recited in claim 23, wherein a polarization angle of the aligning light is selected to specify each orientation of alignment corresponding to the predefined pattern of an associated layer of the three-dimensional (3D) model. The method as recited in claim 24, wherein a minimum difference between the polarization angles of two respective orientations is at least one degree. The method as recited in claim 22, comprising, before exposing the next portion of the layer of photoalignment material to the aligning light, changing a polarity of the aligning light, wherein the changed polarity has a different polarization angle than the previous polarity. The method as recited in claim 22, wherein the next portion of the layer of photoalignment material is positioned by moving the layer of photoalignment material in an x-dircction and/or a y-direction, and the x-y directions are in a plane that is parallel to a plane of the layer of photoalignment material, wherein the aligning light has a projection path in a direction parallel to a z-axis, wherein the next portion of the layer of photoalignment material comprises unaligned molecules. 

The method as recited in claim 22, wherein a wavelength of the aligning light is in a range of greater than about 390 nanometers to about 500 nanometers. The method as recited in claim 22, wherein the photoinitiator does not initiate polymerization of the resin at a wavelength of the aligning light. The method as recited in claim 22, comprising using a digital micromirror device to define the portions of the layer of photoalignmcnt material during exposure to the aligning light. The method as recited in claim 21, wherein the orientation(s) of alignment in each layer of resin corresponds to a predefined pattern of an associated layer of a three- dimensional (3D) model. The method as recited in claim 21, comprising, before forming the at least one additional layer, exposing a next portion of the layer of resin adjacent to a second portion of the photoalignment material to the curing light, wherein the first and second portions of the photoalignment material have different orientations. The method as recited in claim 32, wherein the structure comprises a single layer of resin. The method as recited in claim 21, wherein at least three layers of resin are formed, each layer having a cured portion of cured liquid crystal elastomers, the cured portions being stacked upon one another in a direction perpendicular to a plane of the layers. The method as recited in claim 33, wherein the orientations of the aligned liquid crystal elastomers in the cured portions are different from one another. 

The method as recited in claim 21, wherein a wavelength of the curing light is in a range of greater than about 350 nanometers to about 390 nanometers. The method as recited in claim 21, wherein a composition of the photoalignment material includes at least one component selected from the group consisting of: azobenzene and an organogel. The method as recited in claim 21, comprising heating the resin to a temperature above the nematic -to-isotropic transition temperature of the liquid crystal oligomers; and cooling the resin to a temperature in a nematic range for causing the liquid crystal oligomers to transition from an isotropic state to the nematic state for aligning in an orientation corresponding to the alignment of molecules in the adjacent first portion of the layer of photoalignment material. The method as recited in claim 21, wherein the three-dimensional structure is formed using a lithography-based additive manufacturing system. The method as recited in claim 21, wherein each cured portion comprises at least one voxel. The method as recited in claim 21, wherein the resin includes a photoinhibitor.
